translations.translation:
Lü Qian (Zike)
呂虔 (子恪)
Lived AD late 2nd century–after 226
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 18 (Wei 18)
byZL181
onFebruary 20th, 2025
AbstractEntity::setUpdateForRefresh: 'update_for_refresh' value 'NULL' changed to 'true' via 'loadFlags::$this->update_for_refresh'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'published_at' changed to '1740010500' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'rendered_at' changed to '1758210789' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'updated_at' changed to '1740524250' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'created_at' changed to '1739850933' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
Lü Qian, styledZike, was fromRencheng[任城].
When theGrand Progenitorwas inYan province[兖州], he heard that[Lü] Qianwas valiant and resourceful, and so made him aClerk, who led his household troops to guardHuling[湖陸].Jiong Muand other subordinate people ofDu Song, theColonel1ofXiangling[襄陵校尉], rebelled, and colluded withChang Xi. TheGrand Progenitorhad[Lü] Qianreplace[Du] Song. When[Lü] Qianarrived, he enticed tens of commanders and fellow villains ofJiong Mu, awarding them wine and meals but selecting strong warriors to lay hidden at the sides. When[Lü] Qianobserved thatJiong Mu2and the others were all intoxicated, he had the hidden troops strike and slay them all. He comforted their remaining troops, and so the traitors werepacified.3•••
This may have been aChief Commandanttitle rather than aColoneltitle, forChief Commandanttitles were often assigned to locations whereasColoneltitles rarely focused on locations.↩
Chinese histories tend to omit the surname and only write the forename after introducing the person, henceLü Qianis called "[Lü] Qian” after the first line in the biography.Jiong Muis referred to by his full name even after his first appearance, and so it is unclear ifJiong Muis meant to be referred in the text by his full name, or that his forename is instead Jiongmu, with the preceding character Min [民] before the first instance of Jiongmu being his surname. For this translation, becauseJiong Muis such a minor historical figure used to makeLü Qianseem clever, I have opted for the full name interpretation.↩
The implication is that he would show mercy to the remaining troops if they surrendered, probably since they were only following orders, and surrender they did.↩
TheGrand Progenitormade[Lü] QiantheGrand AdministratorofTaishan[泰山太守]. The commandery was adjacent to mountains and seas, and when the world was in chaos, he heard that many of the people went into hiding. Tens ofHousehold Generals installed byYuan Shao,Guo Zu,Gongsun Du[公孫犢], and many others relied on the mountains to intrude, and the common people suffered from them.[Lü] Qianled his household troops to the commandery and displayed kindness and sincerity, causing[Guo] Zu, the others, and their fellows to all submit in submission. Those who were hiding in the mountains all went out, settled down, and worked on their land. He selected the strong to fill the warrior ranks, and thus,Taishan[泰山] had elite troops, having the best reputation among the provinces and commanderies.
Yellow ScarvesXu HeofJi’nan[濟南] and others kidnapped local senior officials wherever they were and attacked cities.[Lü] Qianled troops to join withXiahou Yuanto attack them. In the tens of battles throughout, they beheaded and captured alive thousands of men. TheGrand Progenitorsent him to supervise the troops of the commanderies inQing province[青州] so as to suppressLi Tiaoand various traitors ofDonglai[東萊], achieving merits. TheGrand Progenitorordered, “When someone has ambition, they will certainly achieve their undertaking. This is probably what stalwart warriors would risk their lives for. Sir, ever since you came to your commandery, you have captured the vile and suppressed the violent, giving security to the common people. You have personally braved arrows and stones, and wherever you led expeditions in, you would always triumph. In the days of yore, Kou Xun [寇恂] established his fame inYing[汝] andRu[潁],4and Geng Yan [耿弇] formulated plans inQing[青] andYan[兖].5How same are the past and present!” He was nominated asOutstanding Talent, additionally madeCavalry Commandant, and directed the commandery as before.[Lü] Qianwas inTaishan[泰山] for tens of years, where he had a great amount of might andgrace.•••
Kou Xun served underGlorious and Martial Emperor. He governed first inRunanin the year 26, then inYingchuanin the year 27. He followed the law, and he successfully led armies to suppress the bandit leaders and their cronies that were causing trouble there.↩
Geng Yan was a notable general underGlorious and Martial Emperor. He suggested to the emperor that he should conquer the lands of Qi, which had the provinces ofQingandYanin it. The emperor agreed and Qi was successfully subjugated.↩
After theCivil Emperorbecame king, he was additionally madeLieutenant Generaland enfeoffed asDistrict Marquisof Yishou [益壽亭侯], then promoted toInspectorofXu province[徐州刺史] and additionally madeGeneral who Shows Might to Caitiffs. He invitedWang XiangofLangye[琅邪] to serve asAssistant Official, entrusting entire domestic affairs onto him. The world praised him for the ability to appoint the virtuous.AHe suppressed the rebelling traitors ofLicheng[利城], gaining merits for decapitations and captures.
TheMiscellaneous Accounts on Similarities and Differencesby Sun Sheng states:[Wang] Xiangwas styledXiuzheng. He was utmost in filial piety by nature; his stepmother, being harsh and cruel, would always want to endanger or harm[Wang] Xiang, but[Wang] Xiangnurtured her with a pleasant expression and without any laze. In the months of frigidity, his stepmother said, “I am thinking about eating fresh fish.” So[Wang] Xiangdiscarded his clothes6and was breaking open the ice to search for one, but did little [effect]. When the ice melted, a fish leapt out. He then presented it as an offering. People at the time believed this result was due to his filial piety moving [the gods]. He supported and nurtured for over thirty years, and when the mother died, he then took office. He was valued in his time for his genuineness, honesty, sincerity,purity.7•••
It seems in this rendition of the story thatWang Xiangintended to use his clothes as a sort of basket to retrieve the fish.↩
This implies thatWang Xiangwaited a very long time for the ice to have melted. Some renditions of the tale go further and state that the ice melted because of his body heat or because he laid on the ice.↩
TheBook of JinbyWang Yinstates: When[Wang] Xiangfirst went and assumed office, he was over fifty years old. He was promoted toColonel who Directs Retainers. When theTownship DukeofGaogui Townshipwas learning, he made[Wang] Xiangserve as Triad Elder. He would be promoted toExcellency of Works, thenGrand Commandant. When theCivil Kingof the Sima clanfirst becameKingof Jin, theExcellency of Works,Xun Yi, wanted[Wang] Xiangto pay his respects to the fullest, but[Wang] Xiangdid not heed him. This is stated in theAnnals of the Three Young Emperors. When theMartial Emperorof Jinascended the throne steps, he conferred[Wang] Xiangto beGrand Protectorand enfeoffed him to beDukeof Juling. In the fourth year of theera, he died aged eighty-nine years old. The younger brother of[Wang] Xiangwas[Wang] Lan, styledXuantong, who served asBrilliantly Blessed Grandee.
TheAppraisal of the Lords of Jinpraises[Wang] Lanof being straightforward and pure, having great conduct. The many descendants of[Wang] Lanhad virtuous talent from generation to generation and being outstanding in their time. From antiquity to the present, few could compare to them.
After theBright Emperorassumed the throne, he shifted fief toDistrict Marquisof Wannian [萬年亭侯], and his fief was increased by two hundred households, joining with the previous for a total of six hundred households. When[Lü] Qiandied, his son,[Lü] Fan, succeeded him. When[Lü] Fandied, his son,[Lü] Gui, succeeded him.
